perturbation.original_text: Western Copper covenants and agrees with Glamis that, for a period of 2 years after the Effective Date, it will not, directly or indirectly, either individually or in partnership or jointly or in conjunction with any Person, which will include being a principal, agent, shareholder, or advisor of such Person or in any other manner whatsoever, (a) carry on or be engaged in Mining Activities, or i 162967.3 -4- (b) advise, lend money to, guarantee the debts or obligations of or permit its name to be used by any Person who carries on or is engaged in Mining Activities, in the Area of Non-Competition.
perturbation.changed_text: Western Copper covenants and agrees with Glamis that, for a period of 2 years after the Effective Date, it will endeavor not to, directly or indirectly, either individually or in partnership or jointly or in conjunction with any Person, which will include being a principal, agent, shareholder, or advisor of such Person or in any other manner whatsoever, (a) carry on or be engaged in Mining Activities, or (b) advise, lend money to, guarantee the debts or obligations of or permit its name to be used by any Person who carries on or is engaged in Mining Activities, in the Area of Non-Competition, to the best of their abilities.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will not' to 'will endeavor not to' and adding 'to the best of their abilities' weakens the obligation, creating ambiguity and allowing for subjective interpretation of compliance. This introduces uncertainty regarding the enforceable standard of non-competition.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract under British Columbia Law
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification weakens the obligation of non-competition. Under British Columbia contract law, covenants, especially non-competition agreements, are interpreted based on the clear intention of the parties. Adding the qualifier 'endeavor not to, to the best of their abilities' undermines the clear intention to prevent competition, making the agreement difficult to enforce if Western Copper takes actions that arguably do not violate the weakened covenant. A court might find the 'best of their abilities' clause too vague to enforce a strict prohibition on competition, especially considering the potential damages Glamis might suffer, which the original contract recognizes as difficult to quantify with monetary value.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 2008 BCSC 132 Bartolome v. Mr. Payday Easy Loans Inc. IN THE SUPREME COURT OF BRITISH COLUMBIA Citation: Bartolome v. Mr. Payday Easy Loans Inc., 2008 BCSC 132 Date: 20080201 Docket: L051079 Registry: Vancouver Between: Jose Bartolome Plaintiff And Mr. Payday Easy Loans Inc. and Pavel Solovev Defendants - and - Docket: L051075 Registry: Vancouver Between: Jose Bartolome Plaintiff And Nationwide Payday Advance Inc. Defendant - and - Docket: S045479 Registry: Vancouver Between: Jose Bartolome Plaintiff And Cashnow Solutions Inc. DBA Cash Converters Guildford Defendant Before: The Honourable Madam Justice Brown Reasons for Judgment Counsel for the plaintiff P.R. Bennett M.W. Mounteer Counsel for the defendant W.K. Branch L. Brasil Date and Place of Hearing: September 17, 2007 Vancouver, B.C. [1] The plaintiff, Mr. Bartolome, applies to certify each of these actions against the defendants. These applications were heard together. The defendants are represented by the same counsel and have each taken the same position in opposition to the application. [2] The factual circumstances of each case against the defendants are somewhat different, so I will deal with these individually at the outset. NATIONWIDE PAYDAY ADVANCE INC. [3] Since April 2000, Nationwide Payday Advance Inc. (Nationwide) has provided short-term loans for small amounts known as Payday Loans, at various locations in British Columbia and by transactions through the internet and by telephone. These loans were for amounts up to $500 and for a term not exceeding 20 days. [4] Nationwides standard form of loan agreement required the borrower to pay: 1. Interest, which was stated to be 59% per annum; and 2. An Administration Fee calculated at 22% of the principal advanced. If the borrower did not attend at the Nationwide location and repay the amount of the Payday Loan, interest fee, and Administration Fee in cash, on or before 12:00 noon the day after the due date, Nationwide would directly debit the borrowers bank account for that amount pursuant to a Payment Authorization Agreement executed by the borrower when the loan was obtained. [5] Between 2003 and 2004, Mr. Bartolome obtained approximately 25 Payday Loans from Nationwide. Each of these loans was for between $200 and $400 and for a term of not more than 15 days. Mr. Bartolome repaid all but the final loan on time and in full. The final loan remains unpaid. [6] The plaintiff provided an expert report from Mr. Ian Karp setting out the actuarial evidence in relation to Mr. Bartolomes dealings with Nationwide. That report indicates that, as an example, Mr. Bartolomes loan of $300 on August 16, 2003, repaid at $372.78 on August 30, 2003, resulted in an effective annual interest rate, if the Administration Fee is included in the calculation of interest, of 28,697%. [7] Mr. Karp further opines that interest alone, which is stated to be calculated at 59% per annum, actually exceeded 60% in each case. [8] Finally, Mr. Karp indicates that an Administrative Fee of 22% of the principal advanced will result in an effective annual rate of interest in excess of 60% where the principal amount of the loan advanced is repaid, with that Administrative Fee, within 154 days of the loan advance. [9] Between February 2002 and January 10, 2007, Nationwide provided loans to 3,047 borrowers in British Columbia. MR. PAYDAY EASY LOANS INC. AND PAVEL SOLOVEV [10] Since February 2002, Mr. Payday Easy Loans Inc. (Mr. Payday) has been in the business of providing and has provided short-term loans for small amounts known as Payday Loans at a location at 3421 Kingsway in Vancouver, British Columbia, and transactions through the internet and by telephone. These loans were for amounts between $100 and $600 for a term not exceeding the borrowers next scheduled pay day. If the borrower wished to obtain a loan immediately, Mr. Paydays standard form loan agreement required: 1. the borrower to pay interest, which prior to November 2002 was stated
perturbation.justification: The change from 'will not' to 'will endeavor not to, to the best of their abilities' weakens the non-competition obligation. The Law Explanation states this undermines the clear intention to prevent competition, making the agreement difficult to enforce. While the scraped snippets do not directly address non-compete clauses, the weakening of a contractual obligation creates an ambiguous legal obligation, as per the Perturbation Type definition. This ambiguity introduces a contradiction because it moves the commitment from a clear prohibition to a subjective standard.

perturbation.original_text: Each of Western Copper and Glamis agree that all restrictions in this Agreement applicable to them are reasonable and valid, and all defences to the strict enforcement thereof by Western Copper or Glamis, as the case may be, arc hereby waived by them.
perturbation.changed_text: Each of Western Copper and Glamis believe that the restrictions in this Agreement applicable to them are generally reasonable and valid, and any non-material defenses to the enforcement thereof by Western Copper or Glamis, as the case may be, are hereby considered waived by them.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'agree' with 'believe', 'are reasonable and valid' with 'are generally reasonable and valid', 'all defenses' with 'any non-material defenses', and 'waived' with 'considered waived' introduces significant ambiguity. The original text represents a clear and unequivocal agreement on the reasonableness and validity of the restrictions and a complete waiver of defenses. The modified text introduces subjectivity and allows for arguments about what constitutes 'generally reasonable' or a 'non-material' defense, weakening the enforceability of the restrictions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Breach of Contract under British Columbia Law - Enforceability of Contractual Terms
perturbation.law_explanation: Under British Columbia contract law, certainty and clarity are crucial for enforceability. A court may refuse to enforce a contract (or specific terms within it) if the terms are too vague or ambiguous. By changing the language to 'believe that the restrictions are generally reasonable' and only waiving 'non-material' defenses, the contract now lacks the necessary certainty. The agreement regarding the reasonableness of the restrictions and waiver of defenses becomes conditional and subjective. This contradicts the principle that contracts should clearly define the obligations of each party, making the restrictive covenants harder to enforce because either party could argue that certain restrictions were not 'generally' reasonable or that a particular defense was 'material,' thereby undermining the intended effect of the clause.
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces ambiguity by changing 'agree' to 'believe,' 'are reasonable and valid' to 'are generally reasonable and valid,' 'all defenses' to 'any non-material defenses,' and 'waived' to 'considered waived.' This creates a significant weakening of the enforceability of the restrictions. The Law Explanation states that under British Columbia contract law, certainty and clarity are crucial for enforceability, and a court may refuse to enforce a contract if the terms are too vague or ambiguous. By introducing subjective language such as 'generally reasonable' and 'non-material,' the contract lacks the necessary certainty, making it harder to enforce. The changes directly contradict the need for clear and definite obligations.
